problem_statement: AI coding assistants (ACAs) can generate code governed by restrictive open-source licenses (e.g., GPL/AGPL), exposing companies to litigation or forced open-sourcing, yet few developers are trained on these risks and most firms lack formal processes to review AI-generated code.
magnitude_evidence: A 2024 GitHub survey reports 97% of developers in large corporations use ACAs; Checkmarx found only 29% of companies had any AI-related governance; a separate survey noted 60% of organizations lack formal processes for assessing ACA-generated code; regurgitation rates found as high as 80% for some ACAs [8].
insufficiency_evidence: Indemnity protections are 'often subject to caveats' and content filters 'have been shown to be easily circumvented [9]'; 67% of developers spend more time debugging/modifying buggy ACA code (which breaks indemnity); merely altering a few lines is 'unlikely to be sufficient' to escape copyright on the 'heart' of the code.
solution_mechanisms: SCA license scanning in CI/CD (SCANOSS, FOSSA, Black Duck)
block/flag code pushes on critical violations
periodic manual audits by trained experts
customized/safe ACAs trained on permissive code (StarCoder, InCoder)
governance_refs: Orange S.A. GPL ruling (Court of Appeal of Paris)
